text: The patient is positioned in a right semilateral decubitus position. Pneumoperitoneum is established in the right upper quadrant, and four additional trocars are placed. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to divide adhesions and expose the inferior splenic pole, assisted by ultramatic graspers. The spleen is gently lifted with a blunt retractor, revealing a large intraparenchymal hematoma without free fluid. The splenocolic ligament is divided using a monopolar hook, and bleeding from minor vessels or the splenic capsule is controlled with bipolar energy.

The surgeon tilts the spleen medially to expose and divide the splenorenal ligament using a monopolar hook. The lateral side of the splenophrenic ligament is also divided with the monopolar hook, taking care to avoid diaphragmatic injury. The spleen is then tilted laterally to expose the splenic hilum, gastrosplenic ligament, and medial side of the splenophrenic ligament. The splenophrenic ligament division is completed, and the splenic hilum is approached from caudal to cranial by lifting the inferior splenic pole with a blunt retractor. The monopolar hook is used to open the peritoneal surface of the splenic hilum using the hook, look, and cook technique.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to control minor vessels, which are then divided with a hook. Careful dissection exposes the splenic hilar vessels. Major vessels are dissected and prepared using Maryland and right angle forceps. The splenic main artery, smaller due to celiac trunk stenosis, is secured with a haemolog clip on the proximal side and further secured by an intracorporeal tie.

The surgeon places an intracorporeal tie on the distal side of the splenic hilum to facilitate quick stapling or clamping in case of sudden major bleeding. The vessel is sharply divided, and further dissection with Maryland forceps and a hook exposes the inferior splenic pole vessels. The inferior polar vein and artery are clearly visible and are subsequently clipped, tied, and sharply divided.

The surgeon observes transient bleeding from a large intraparenchymal hematoma following the ligation of a major splenic vein, which is managed by packing. The bleeding ceases after clipping the main artery. The hilar dissection continues using monopolar hook and bipolar energy, dissecting, clipping, and sharply dividing a branch of the main splenic artery and the splenic hilar vein. Hem-o-locks are placed on both sides, and the hyaluronate division is completed using monopolar and bipolar cautery.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels using a vascular endostapler, ensuring hemostasis by waiting before firing. Bipolar energy refines hemostasis, and the spleen specimen is retrieved in a large endo bag through the left upper quadrant incision by morcellation. The surgical field is cleaned and inspected, with careful checks on the hemostasis of the splenic hilum and short gastric vessels, and haemostatic sealant is applied.
